claims.evidence_text: Cold period (=vernalization) accelerates the flowering of winter (autumn-sown) varieties. The length of effective vernalization can range from three to eight weeks (Košner, Pánková 2002; Li et al., 2013).
claims.claim_summary: Effective vernalization length for winter wheat ranges from 3 to 8 weeks (~21-56 days).

claims.evidence_text: Cold and short days during winter downregulate VRN2, releasing both VRN1 and FT1 transcription. Vernalization is a period of low non-freezing temperatures, which provides the competence to flower.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat requires a period of low non-freezing (cold) temperatures plus short days to acquire flowering competence; vernalization requirement is genotype-dependent.

claims.evidence_text: Depending on the growth conditions, the spring wheat heading time can range from 25 to 90 days (Li et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2018).
claims.claim_summary: Spring wheat heading time ranges from 25 to 90 days depending on growth conditions.

claims.evidence_text: The effect of vernalization treatment can be partially or completely removed... by several days of heat treatment called devernalization. The most effective temperature was considered 30 – 40°C (Bernier, 1981).
claims.claim_summary: Heat treatment of 30-40 °C is most effective at devernalizing (reversing vernalization in) wheat.

claims.evidence_text: This ability is related to a variation in the two main genes: PPD1 (PHOTOPERIOD1, photoperiod response) and VRN1 (VERNALIZATION1, vernalization requirement). In autumn-sown winter wheat, the flowering induction is repressed by the VRN2 gene during long days
claims.claim_summary: Wheat flowering time and adaptation governed by photoperiod response (PPD1) and vernalization requirement (VRN1); long days repress flowering in winter wheat via VRN2.
